---
title: "ETO5513-A1"
author: "Eileen Koh"
date: "2026-03-15"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    keep_md: true
---


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo=FALSE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.path = "Figures", fig.align ="center", out.width = "80%")
#options(bookdown.table.caption = FALSE)
```

# Part A

## Load libraries


```{r Load_libraries, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE,   warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE)

library(knitr)
library(markdown)
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)  #Plots
library(dplyr)
library(bookdown)
library(kableExtra)
```

## Research Questions

1. What are the life expectancy and life satisfaction in Asia countries? 
2. Is life expectancy correlated with life satisfaction?



## Dataset Introduction


This dataset from Our World in Data website ["Life satisfaction vs. life expectancy"] (https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/life-satisfaction-vs-life-expectancy)  shows the life expectancies at birth, population size and self-reported life satisfaction across countries from 2011 to 2024. The original dataset has been reduced to include only selected Asia countries to fit the criteria of the assessment. 

The self-reported life satisfaction is defined when the respondents are supposed to think of a ladder, with the best possible life for them being a 10 and the worst possible life being a 0. They are then asked to rate their own current lives on that 0 to 10 scale.

### Exploring dataset


```{r Load_data}
life_df <- readxl::read_excel("C:/Users/eleen/Documents/Documents/Monash Units/TP2-26_ETO5513 Collaborative/A1/ETO5513-A1/Data/Life Expectancy and Life Satisfaction_Asia.xlsx")
```

```{r Explore_data}
head(life_df)
glimpse(life_df)
summary(life_df)
# use kable to report variables
```

### Table 1: List of variables in dataset 

```{r var-table}
knitr::kable(
  data.frame(Variable = names(life_df)),
  caption = "List of variables in the dataset"
)
```


As shown in Table \@ref(tab:var-table), the dataset contains several variables.

## Dataset Description

The dataset comprises `r nrow(life_df)` observations and `r ncol(life_df)` variables covering `r paste(names(life_df), collapse = ", ")` across Asian countries between 2011 and 2024.

Below shows the image of the inline R code on the number of variables and observations.

```{r Load_Image}
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/eleen/Documents/Documents/Monash Units/TP2-26_ETO5513 Collaborative/A1/ETO5513 Assessment 1/Picture1.png")
```

Below shows the type of variables in the data set. There are 4 numeric variables and 1 character variable.
```{r Showtype}

str(life_df)
```

## Data Summary

1. What are the life expectancy and life satisfaction in different countries in Asia? 

**Analysis on Life Expectancy**

### Summary Statistics of Life Expectancy in Asia countries 

```{r Life_expectancy_summary, tab.cap="Summary Statistics of Life Expectancy in Asia countries"}

# 1. Life expectancy in Asia countries 

# Generate mean and median of life expectancy
summary_stats <- life_df %>%
  group_by(Entity) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    `Mean of Life Expectancy` = mean(`Life expectancy at birth`, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Variance of Life Expectancy` = var(`Life expectancy at birth`, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Median of Life Expectancy` = median(`Life expectancy at birth`, na.rm = TRUE),
    `1st quarter of Life Expectancy` = quantile(`Life expectancy at birth`, probs = 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    `3rd quarter of Life Expectancy` = quantile(`Life expectancy at birth`, probs = 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
print(summary_stats)
```

```{r table2}
# Put into a table using kable

kable(
  data.frame(summary_stats),
  caption = "Summary Statistics of Life Expectancy in Asia countries"
)
```




Table \@ref(tab:table2) shows that countries such as Hong Kong (84.3) have the highest mean life expectancy, while **India** has the lowest among the listed Asian countries. Overall, the variation in life expectancy within each country is relatively small, suggesting stable health outcomes over time.


**Analysis for Life Satisfaction** 

### Summary Statistics of Life Satisfaction in Asia countries 

```{r Life_satisfaction_summary}

# 2. Life satisfaction in Asia countries


summary_stats2 <- life_df %>%
  group_by(Entity) %>%
    summarise(
    n = n(),
    `Mean of Life Satisfaction` = mean(`Life satisfaction (measured from lowest=0 to highest=10 on Cantril Ladder)`, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Variance of Life Satisfaction` = var(`Life satisfaction (measured from lowest=0 to highest=10 on Cantril Ladder)`, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Median of Life Satisfaction` = median(`Life satisfaction (measured from lowest=0 to highest=10 on Cantril Ladder)`, na.rm = TRUE),
    `1st quarter of Life Satisfaction` = quantile(`Life satisfaction (measured from lowest=0 to highest=10 on Cantril Ladder)`, probs = 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    `3rd quarter of Life Satisfaction` = quantile(`Life satisfaction (measured from lowest=0 to highest=10 on Cantril Ladder)`, probs = 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
print(summary_stats2)
```

```{r table3}
kable(
  data.frame(summary_stats2), 
  caption = "Summary Statistics of Life Satisfaction in Asia countries"
)

```


Table \@ref(tab:table3) indicates that Singapore (6.5 out of 10) reports the highest mean life satisfaction scores, while **India** shows the lowest among the Asian countries listed. Overall, the variance within each country is small, suggesting that life satisfaction levels are fairly consistent across the observed years.




## Visualisations



To answer the research question on "Is life expectancy correlated with life satisfaction?", a scatterplot is created below.

```{r chart, fig.cap="Correlation between Life Expectancy and Life Satisfaction"}
ggplot(life_df, aes(x = `Life expectancy at birth`, y = `Life satisfaction (measured from lowest=0 to highest=10 on Cantril Ladder)`, color = `Life satisfaction (measured from lowest=0 to highest=10 on Cantril Ladder)`)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "blue", high = "red") +
  labs(title = "Correlation between Life expectancy and Life Satisfaction",
       x = "Life Expectancy at Birth",
       y = "Life Satisfaction (0=low to 10=high)",
       color = "Life Satisfaction") +
  theme_minimal()
  

```

To determine if the relationship between Life Expectancy and Life Satisfaction is significant, the Pearson Correlation is performed.

```{r Correlation}

pearson_corr <- cor.test(life_df$`Life expectancy at birth`, life_df$`Life satisfaction (measured from lowest=0 to highest=10 on Cantril Ladder)`, method = "pearson")
print(pearson_corr)

```
Findings: 

  + *From the above scatterplot (Figure \@ref(fig:chart)) and correlation analysis, there is a moderately strong positive correlation (r ≈ 0.60) between life expectancy and life satisfaction, meaning that countries with higher life expectancy tend to also report higher life satisfaction.*

  + *The confidence interval (0.47 to 0.71) and low p-value (<0.05) indicate that this relationship is statistically significant, not due to random chance.*




# Conclusion

The findings reflect on the variation in Asian countries in both life expectancy and life satisfaction: countries like Japan, Hong Kong, and Singapore rank high in both life expectancy and life satisfaction, while India consistently reports lower values.

The statistically significant positive correlation (r ≈ 0.60) between life expectancy and life satisfaction indicates that those living longer life tend to report greater well-being.

Together, these findings answer the two guiding questions: life expectancy and life satisfaction differ across Asia, and importantly, higher life expectancy is correlated with higher life satisfaction.




## References


[1] United Nations. (2024). World Population Prospects – Life Expectancy, age 0 [dataset]. Processed by Our World in Data. Retrieved from https://population.un.org/wpp/downloads/


[2] Wellbeing Research Centre. (2025). Self-reported life satisfaction – World Happiness Report 2025 [dataset]. Processed by Our World in Data. Retrieved from https://worldhappiness.report/ed/2025/

[3] PBL Netherlands Environmental Assessment Agency, Gapminder, & United Nations. (2023–2024). Population – HYDE, Gapminder, UN – Long-run data [dataset]. Processed by Our World in Data. Retrieved from https://ourworldindata.org/population-sources (ourworldindata.org in Bing)

[4] Our World in Data. (2023). World region according to OWID [dataset]. Retrieved from https://ourworldindata.org


## Declaration

I acknowledge the use of ChatGPT (https://chat.openai.com/) to debug errors in code and enhancing language or grammar.
